extracted_information: The document provides a comprehensive overview of Kubernetes multi-tenancy, including different models, security patterns, isolation techniques for both control plane and data plane, and best practices. It differentiates between multi-team and multi-customer tenancy models and details various Kubernetes features and configurations to achieve desired levels of isolation and resource management in shared clusters.

multi_tenancy_models.description: Sharing a cluster between multiple teams within an organization. Workloads frequently communicate. Members often have direct `kubectl` access or indirect via GitOps/automation. Policies like RBAC, quotas, and network policies are essential.
characteristics: Teams typically deploy small number of workloads
Assumes some level of trust between teams
Relies on RBAC, Quotas, Network Policies for safe sharing

multi_tenancy_models.description: SaaS vendor running multiple instances of a workload for customers. Customers do not have cluster access. Kubernetes is used by the vendor for workload management. Cost optimization and strong isolation are critical concerns.
characteristics: Typically for external customers (e.g., SaaS providers)
Customers do not have direct access to the cluster
Cost optimization is a critical concern
Strong isolation required due to untrusted tenants

multi_tenancy_models.description: Combination of multi-team and multi-customer models, or per-customer workloads with shared services.

isolation_types.description: Implies strong isolation, often used when tenants do not trust each other (security, resource sharing, guarding against data exfiltration/DoS). Requires extra attention to data-plane isolation, with control plane isolation also critical.
Assigning each tenant their dedicated cluster, possibly on dedicated hardware. Provides strongest isolation but at higher cost and complexity. Easier with managed Kubernetes clusters.

implementation_approaches.description: Isolating each workload or tenant within its own Kubernetes Namespace. Well-supported by Kubernetes, negligible resource cost, allows service-to-service communication. Can be difficult to configure and doesn't apply to non-namespaced resources.
disadvantages: Can be difficult to configure properly
Doesn't apply to non-namespaced Kubernetes resources (CRDs, Storage Classes, Webhooks)
Challenges with tenant-level vs. workload-level namespace assignment
Difficulty in customizing policies for individual workloads when tenant-scoped
best_practices: Isolate each workload in its own namespace
Assign unique names to namespaces across the entire fleet
Consider hierarchical namespaces for policy and resource sharing

implementation_approaches.description: Provides each tenant with a virtual control plane, extending namespace-based multi-tenancy by offering dedicated control plane components (API server, controller manager, etcd). Worker nodes are shared. Solves control plane isolation issues but adds operational overhead.
advantages: Dedicated control plane components per tenant
Complete control over cluster-wide resources and add-on services
Solves control plane noisy neighbors, policy misconfigurations, and cluster scope object conflicts
Suitable when each tenant requires Kubernetes API access
disadvantages: Higher resource usage compared to namespace isolation
Cost of running and maintaining an individual virtual control plane per tenant
Does not solve data plane isolation problems (node-level noisy neighbors, security threats)
implementation_approaches.interaction: Interacts with a 'super-cluster' (host-cluster) via a metadata synchronization controller.

features.name: Namespaces
features.description: Mechanism for isolating groups of API resources. Provides isolation for object names and scope for security policies (RBAC, Network Policies). Helps segment tenant workloads into logical management units. Requires configuration of other resources, networking plugins, and security best practices for proper isolation.

features.name: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
features.description: Used to enforce authorization for users and workloads (service accounts). Critical for ensuring tenants have appropriate access (Principle of Least Privilege). Roles and RoleBindings are namespace-scoped, while ClusterRoles/ClusterRoleBindings are for cluster-level access.
features.details: Restricts tenants' access to appropriate namespaces; cluster-wide resources accessed/modified only by privileged users.

features.name: Resource Quotas
features.description: Manages resource usage (CPU, memory, API resources count) for tenant workloads. Prevents 'noisy neighbor' issues by ensuring fair resource allocation. Namespace-scoped, can be extended by namespace management tools to share quotas across groups of namespaces. Requires resource requests and limits for containers.
features.impact: Minimizes 'noisy neighbor' where one tenant monopolizes resources; doesn't protect against all resource sharing issues like network traffic.

features.name: Network Policies
features.description: Restricts communication between pods using namespace labels or IP address ranges. Recommended to start with a default deny policy and explicitly allow necessary communication (e.g., DNS).
advanced_options: Service meshes: Provide OSI Layer 7 policies based on workload identity, encryption (mTLS), and work across dedicated/virtual clusters.

features.name: Dynamic Volume Provisioning
features.description: Recommended for storage isolation and security. Avoid volume types that use node resources. Paired with StorageClasses and PersistentVolumeClaims.
features.recommendation: Set reclaim policy of `Delete` for shared StorageClasses to prevent reuse across namespaces.

features.name: StorageClasses
features.description: Allows defining custom classes of storage based on QoS, backup policies, or custom admin policies. Can be configured separately for each tenant to strengthen isolation. Used in conjunction with PersistentVolumeClaim.
features.usage: Tenant requests storage using a PersistentVolumeClaim (namespaced resource).

features.name: Sandboxing Containers
features.description: Provides stronger isolation than OS-level virtualization by running pods in separate execution environments (VMs, userspace kernels). Recommended for untrusted code or to insulate the host from container escapes. Addresses shared kernel and file system issues (e.g., /sys, /proc).
features.alternative_controls: seccomp, AppArmor, SELinux can strengthen container security but are hard to apply universally.

features.name: Node Isolation
features.description: Dedicates a set of nodes to run pods from a particular tenant, prohibiting co-mingling. Reduces 'noisy tenant' issues and lowers information disclosure risk. Kubelet and API service remain shared. Can be implemented using taints/tolerations, mutating webhooks, and pod node selectors.
considerations: Fewer compatibility and performance issues than sandboxing containers
Requires compensating controls (seccomp, AppArmor, SELinux) if shared services are a concern

features.name: API Priority and Fairness
features.description: Kubernetes feature to assign priority to pods' API calls. Higher priority calls fulfilled first. Lower priority calls can be queued or rejected. Not common in SaaS unless customers interact with Kubernetes API directly.
features.usage_context: Mainly relevant when allowing customers to run applications that interface with the Kubernetes API (e.g., a controller).

features.name: Quality-of-Service (QoS) Tiers
features.description: Ability to offer different service tiers with varying performance guarantees. Achieved using network QoS, storage classes, and pod priority and preemption.
mechanisms: Network QoS: Kubernetes bandwidth plugin (experimental) for rate limits using Linux tc queues.
Storage QoS: Create different StorageClasses with varied performance characteristics (IO, redundancy, throughput).
Pod Priority and Preemption: Assign priority values to pods; scheduler evicts lower priority pods for higher priority ones when resources are insufficient.

features.name: DNS Configuration
features.description: By default, Kubernetes DNS allows lookups across all namespaces. For stronger isolation, cross-namespace DNS lookups can be restricted (e.g., using CoreDNS metadata policy). For Virtual Control Plane models, a per-tenant or multi-tenant DNS service is needed.

features.name: Operators
features.description: Kubernetes controllers managing applications. Simplify management of multiple application instances (e.g., database). In multi-tenant environments, operators should support creating resources in tenant namespaces, configure resource requests/limits for pods, and support data-plane isolation techniques.
guidelines: Support creating resources within different tenant namespaces
Ensure Pods are configured with resource requests and limits
Support configuration of Pods for data-plane isolation techniques (node isolation, sandboxed containers)
